summary: DeVault argues that programmers should take full advantage of open source by fearlessly diving into unfamiliar codebases to debug and fix problems at their source. He observes that many developers who are comfortable contributing to one project balk at tracing bugs into dependencies like libinput or mesa, even though the same debugging tools and skills apply everywhere. He makes the case that reading code in unfamiliar languages and libraries is always tractable, and that the biggest time sink is the anxiety about doing it rather than the work itself. The post concludes that effective programmers make it their business to understand the tools they use at every level of the stack.
key_points: Open source gives us the extraordinary privilege of reading and patching every layer of our software stack, and we should actually use it
The intimidation people feel toward unfamiliar codebases is the same fear they once had about projects they now contribute to comfortably
Standard debugging tools like gdb, git grep, and git blame work just as well on dependencies as on your own code — send a patch instead of a workaround
You don't need to be an expert in a language to fix a bug in it; learning just enough to understand a particular problem is always possible
The biggest time sink is worrying about the time investment, not the actual work of reading and understanding foreign code
money_quotes: We live in a golden age of open source, and it can sometimes be easy to forget the privileges that this affords us.
These projects don't just make their code available - they accept patches, too! Why wouldn't we take advantage of this tremendous opportunity?
These tools might seem foreign and scary - but to these people, at some point, so did Sway.
When you find the problem, don't be afraid to send a patch over instead of working around it in your own code.
You don't have to be an expert in a particular programming language to invoke trial & error.
The biggest time sink in this process is all the time you spend worrying about how much time it's going to take.
An important pastime of the effective programmer is reading and understanding the tools you use.
key_insight: The privilege of open source is wasted if programmers are too intimidated to trace problems through unfamiliar codebases and send patches upstream.
